contexts.title: Daily habits
contexts.description: Routines at home, school, and personal life.

contexts.title: IT work
contexts.description: Software, support, meetings, and technical work.

contexts.title: Family routines
contexts.description: Daily activities with family and children.

contexts.title: Travel and vacation
contexts.description: Trips, plans, hotels, and free time.

contexts.title: Prepositions
contexts.description: Place, time, and movement words used in examples.

units.title: Present Simple Foundation
units.focus: Use Present Simple for habits, routines, facts, and schedules.
objectives: Recognize when Present Simple is the right tense.
Build affirmative, negative, and question forms.

sections.title: Meaning
body: Present Simple describes habits, routines, general truths, permanent situations, and schedules.
It usually answers the question: what do you usually do?

structures.pattern: Subject + base verb (+ s/es for he/she/it)
structures.example: He works as an IT engineer.
He does not work on Sundays.

mistakes.why: He/she/it needs -s in affirmative Present Simple.

mistakes.wrong: He does not works on Sundays.
mistakes.right: He does not work on Sundays.
mistakes.why: Does already carries the third-person marker, so the main verb returns to base form.

examples.sentence: Dario checks his email before he starts coding.
examples.note: This describes a regular work routine.

examples.sentence: He drops off his kids at school every morning.
examples.note: Every morning signals a repeated habit.

examples.sentence: She joins the planning meeting every Tuesday.
examples.note: Every Tuesday shows a regular schedule.

examples.sentence: They cook dinner together on Fridays.
examples.note: On Fridays points to a repeated family routine.

examples.sentence: We book a hotel before every vacation.
examples.note: Before every vacation describes a repeated travel habit.

examples.sentence: The laptop stays on the desk during the day.
examples.note: On the desk uses a place preposition in a simple fact.

vocabulary.example: The team deploys the app on Friday.
They have a stand-up every morning.
He picks up his kids after school.

vocabulary.meaning: to put things in a bag before a trip
vocabulary.example: We pack our bags before vacation.

vocabulary.meaning: a word for something that happens most of the time
a preposition for days and surfaces
vocabulary.example: The report is on the desk.

exercises.explanation: Dario is he, so the verb work becomes works.

exercises.prompt: Change to a question: He works in IT.
exercises.explanation: Use does + subject + base verb for he/she/it questions.

exercises.prompt: Choose the tense: Dario checks his email every morning.
exercises.explanation: Every morning signals a repeated routine, so Simple Present is the best tense.

exercises.prompt: She _____ the planning meeting every Tuesday.
exercises.explanation: She needs the -s form in affirmative Present Simple.

exercises.prompt: He _____ up his kids after school.
exercises.explanation: He needs the -s form: picks up.

units.title: Past, Future and Conditional Foundation
units.focus: Use time markers and sequence markers to choose and build past, future, and conditional forms.
objectives: Differentiate past, future, and conditional meaning in short situations.
Use simple, continuous, and perfect forms when describing sequence and duration.
Build affirmative, negative, and question forms with clean subject-verb order.
Avoid common mistakes with tense choices and auxiliary placement.

sections.title: Meaning and usage
body: Past forms describe actions before now and usually need clear time order.
Future forms are used for plans, predictions, and formal expectations.
Conditional forms describe possible situations and hypothetical outcomes.
Perfect and continuous forms help show results and ongoing duration.

structures.pattern: Subject + tense auxiliary pattern + base/participle/gerund as required
structures.example: She had completed the report before the call.

structures.pattern: Subject + auxiliary + not + base/participle/gerund as required
structures.example: They will not be working on the weekend.
Will he be arriving on Friday?

mistakes.wrong: He have worked when I arrived.
mistakes.right: He had worked when I arrived.
mistakes.why: Past perfect needs had + past participle.

mistakes.wrong: If you would came, we would win.
mistakes.right: If you came, we would win.
mistakes.why: After if for first conditional, keep simple past in clause + conditional in result.

mistakes.wrong: She will meet you tomorrow if she have time.
mistakes.right: She will meet you tomorrow if she has time.
mistakes.why: The conditional clause can use present for scheduling or hypothetical patterns.

examples.sentence: She had finished the deployment before they started testing.
examples.note: Past perfect shows an earlier completed action.

examples.sentence: He will be presenting when the meeting starts.
examples.note: Future continuous for action in progress at a future point.

examples.sentence: If he had more free time, he would travel more often.
By next month, we will have planned the whole itinerary.
examples.note: Future perfect for completed action before a future time.

examples.sentence: They were working on the project all day.
examples.note: Past continuous with a long ongoing action.

vocabulary.meaning: a time by which work should be finished
used for limit points in time
vocabulary.example: By the time she called, I had left.
If I had time, I would join.

exercises.prompt: By 8 a.m. she _____ already left the office.
exercises.explanation: Past perfect is used for the action done before a past reference point.

exercises.prompt: Change to negative: He is working on the report now.
exercises.answer: He is not working on the report now.
exercises.explanation: Insert the negative after auxiliary: is not + present participle.

exercises.prompt: Choose the tense: By next year, we ____ the client proposal.
exercises.explanation: Future perfect expresses an action completed before a future time.

exercises.prompt: Correct this sentence: If you would came earlier, we would talk now.
exercises.answer: If you came earlier, we would talk now.
exercises.explanation: After if, the hypothetical clause uses simple past in this pattern.

exercises.prompt: Translate to English: Si ella tuviera tiempo, viajaría más.
exercises.answer: If she had time, she would travel more.
exercises.explanation: Use if + past form in condition and would + base verb in result.
